schema_notes: Each user file powers one instance of the Molbak's Garden Guide prototype. To create a new demo: duplicate this file, update user/spaces/plants/assessment from the OpenClaw output, and point the app at the new file.

user.name: Morten
user.archetype_description: Design-conscious gardener who thinks about color contrast, texture variation, and year-round structure. Good taste in plants with mixed success in execution — outdoor landscape is stronger than indoor collection.

assessment.one_thing: Do a scratch test on the weeping maple in the parking strip — scratch the bark on bare branches with your fingernail. Green underneath means it's alive. Brown means it's time to let go.
assessment.big_picture: You have good taste in plants and a clear design vision — the black fence, black planters, and architectural plant choices tell a cohesive story. Your outdoor landscape is strong: the upright Japanese maple is a showstopper, and the nandina is perfectly placed. The indoor plants need more attention — the orchid is in survival mode and the Bird of Paradise is in the wrong spot. The weeping maple in the parking strip is the one real concern: it may be past saving, and an honest assessment now saves months of false hope.

lifestyle_tip.label: Weekend idea
lifestyle_tip.text: Your Japanese maple's coral-red spring foliage is at peak color right now. Clip a few small branches for a striking tabletop arrangement — they last about a week in water and look gorgeous against your black interior palette.

weeks.label: This week
weeks.tasks: Scratch test maple, cut orchid spikes

spaces.name: Windowsill
spaces.status_detail: Orchid in post-bloom recovery — needs dead spike removal
spaces.coach_note: The orchid is in survival mode but not dying — the leaves are firm and green, which is a good sign. Cut the dead spikes, check the roots, and give it 6–12 months. Patience is the whole game with orchids.

spaces.name: Stairwell
spaces.status_detail: Bird of Paradise is struggling — wrong location for this plant
spaces.coach_note: Real talk: Birds of Paradise are outdoor plants pretending to be houseplants. This one needs bright direct sun, high humidity, and room to grow. The stairwell isn't giving it any of that. Move it to your brightest window, or better yet, put it on your patio May through September. If you want something that actually thrives here, a Monstera or Fiddle Leaf Fig would be much happier.

spaces.name: Front landscape bed
spaces.status_detail: Nandina and upright maple thriving — watch for afternoon heat stress
spaces.coach_note: This is the strongest part of your garden. The upright maple is the focal point — beautiful coral-red spring foliage, good branch structure, well-scaled for the space. The nandina's red new growth against the black fence is striking. Just keep an eye on afternoon sun and soil moisture as summer heats up.
spaces.rearrangement_tip: The nandina is well placed against the fence — keep it there. If the weeping maple doesn't recover, consider replacing it with a dwarf conifer or ornamental grass that can handle parking strip conditions.

spaces.name: Front entry planter
spaces.status_detail: Dwarf conifers looking good — plan for future sizing
spaces.coach_note: The chartreuse conifer against the black planter is a great design choice — you clearly think about color contrast. Just know these will outgrow this container in 2–3 years. Plan ahead: move them to the ground, upsize the pot, or treat them as temporary and replace when they get too big.

plants.name: Orchid
plants.status_detail: Post-bloom recovery — survival mode, not thriving
plants.diagnosis: Flower spikes are spent and need removal. Only 3–4 leaves remaining — healthy orchids typically have 6+. Leaves look firm and green, which is encouraging. This orchid has been through stress but the fundamentals are intact.
plants.real_talk: This orchid is in survival mode, not thriving. The bare flower stems need to go. If the roots are healthy, it can rebloom in 6–12 months. If roots are mushy, it needs repotting immediately.

recovery_plan.title: Cut the dead spikes
recovery_plan.description: Snip them at the base with sterile scissors. They're not reblooming from those stems.

recovery_plan.title: Check roots
recovery_plan.description: Gently remove from pot. Healthy roots are green/silver and firm. Brown/mushy = root rot — trim and repot.

recovery_plan.title: Repot if needed
recovery_plan.description: Fresh orchid bark mix, same pot size or slightly smaller. Don't oversize the pot.

recovery_plan.title: Watering routine
recovery_plan.description: Soak weekly, let drain completely. Never let it sit in water. That filtered window light is perfect — keep it there.

plants.recovery_timeline: 6–12 months to rebloom if roots are healthy. Patience required.

plants.name: Bird of Paradise
plants.status_detail: Wrong location — this is an outdoor plant forced indoors
plants.diagnosis: Brown, crispy leaf edges from low humidity and inconsistent watering. Tattered leaves — possibly from physical contact with walls. Leggy growth reaching for light. No new growth visible, which means it's stalled.
plants.real_talk: Birds of Paradise are outdoor plants pretending to be houseplants. They want bright direct sun (6+ hours), high humidity, and room to grow — they hit 6–8 feet indoors, 20+ outdoors. The stairwell gives it none of that. If you want low-maintenance indoor greenery, a Monstera or Fiddle Leaf Fig would be much happier here.

recovery_plan.title: Move to brightest window
recovery_plan.description: South or west-facing, as close to glass as possible. This is the single biggest thing you can do.

recovery_plan.title: Increase humidity
recovery_plan.description: Pebble tray, humidifier, or group with other plants. This plant needs moisture in the air.

recovery_plan.title: Trim damaged leaves
recovery_plan.description: Cut the worst ones at the base to redirect energy to new growth.

recovery_plan.title: Water deeply
recovery_plan.description: When top 2" dry, soak until water drains. Empty saucer — never let it sit in water.

recovery_plan.title: Consider summer outdoors
recovery_plan.description: This plant would love a patio spot May through September. That's its natural habitat.

plants.recovery_timeline: Visible improvement in 4–6 weeks if relocated. Full recovery takes a growing season.

plants.name: Nandina
plants.status_detail: Beautiful red-orange new growth, full bushy habit, well-established
plants.diagnosis: Classic spring nandina color. Full, bushy habit, good size, well-established. Gravel mulch is appropriate for drainage. The red new growth against the black fence is a striking design choice.
plants.real_talk: Nandina is borderline invasive in the PNW. It spreads by rhizomes and birds eat the berries, spreading seeds. That said, this is a well-contained landscape specimen — just watch for unwanted spread.

recovery_plan.title: Prune after flowering
recovery_plan.description: Remove oldest canes at ground level to renew growth and keep it shapely.

recovery_plan.title: Watch for berries
recovery_plan.description: If it fruits, remove berries before birds get them — invasive concern in PNW.

recovery_plan.title: Check for scale
recovery_plan.description: Nandina gets scale insects. Look for sticky residue or sooty mold on leaves.

plants.name: Japanese maple (upright)
plants.status_detail: Beautiful coral-red spring foliage, good branch structure, well-scaled
plants.diagnosis: Beautiful coral-red new foliage — classic spring color for many varieties. Good branch structure with well-pruned, open canopy. Appropriately sized for the space. This is the focal point of the garden.

recovery_plan.title: Protect from afternoon sun
recovery_plan.description: That gravel bed + south/west exposure = heat stress risk. Consider light afternoon shade in summer.

recovery_plan.title: Keep soil moist
recovery_plan.description: Maples hate drying out. Gravel mulch helps, but check soil moisture weekly in summer.

recovery_plan.title: No pruning now
recovery_plan.description: Wait until dormant season (late fall/winter) for any structural pruning.

recovery_plan.title: Watch for leaf scorch
recovery_plan.description: Brown edges in summer = too much sun or dry soil. Address immediately.

care_info.light_needs: Partial sun, protect from hot afternoon

plants.name: Japanese maple (weeping)
plants.status_detail: Sparse canopy, dead branches, struggling in parking strip conditions
plants.diagnosis: Very sparse leaf coverage — should be much fuller by early April. Some dead branches visible in the canopy. The parking strip location is brutal: gravel/poor soil, full exposure, possible root compaction from foot traffic, and winter cold snaps. This tree is struggling.
plants.real_talk: Weeping maples are beautiful but demanding. This parking strip location — gravel, full exposure, compacted soil — is challenging for any maple. The hard truth: this tree may not make it. Do the scratch test first. If most branches are alive, it's worth trying to save. If not, a fresh start with a tougher species might be the kinder choice.

recovery_plan.title: Scratch test
recovery_plan.description: Scratch bark on bare branches with your fingernail. Green underneath = alive. Brown = dead, prune it out.

recovery_plan.title: Deep water weekly
recovery_plan.description: Parking strips dry out fast. Give it a long, slow soak once a week through summer.

recovery_plan.title: Add wood mulch
recovery_plan.description: 2–3" of wood mulch (not gravel) around the base to retain moisture and regulate soil temperature.

recovery_plan.title: Fertilize lightly
recovery_plan.description: Compost or slow-release fertilizer in spring to support recovery.

recovery_plan.title: Monitor and decide
recovery_plan.description: If no improvement by June, consider replacement. Sometimes a fresh start is better than nursing a failing tree.

plants.recovery_timeline: If alive, visible improvement by June. If no change by midsummer, consider replacing.

care_info.light_needs: Partial shade preferred, full sun tolerable
care_info.soil: Rich, moist, amended — opposite of current gravel

plants.name: Dwarf golden conifer
plants.status_detail: Bright chartreuse color, good shape, well-placed for color contrast
plants.diagnosis: Healthy, bright chartreuse color. Good contrast against the black planter — clearly an intentional design choice. Currently well-sized for the container.
plants.real_talk: This conifer will outgrow the container in 2–3 years. Plan ahead: either move it to the ground, root prune and upsize the pot, or treat it as temporary and replace when it gets too big.

care_info.watering: Regular, containers dry faster than ground

plants.name: Dwarf green conifer
plants.status_detail: Good texture contrast with the golden conifer
plants.diagnosis: Healthy, good form. Provides texture variation paired with the chartreuse conifer — fine foliage vs. chunky globe. Same container outgrowth timeline applies.

soil_profiles.label: Landscape bed
soil_profiles.description: Native soil with gravel mulch top — well-draining, possibly lean

soil_profiles.label: Parking strip
soil_profiles.description: Compacted gravel/soil mix — poor conditions, needs amendment

soil_profiles.label: Containers
soil_profiles.description: Bark mulch over container mix — standard, well-draining

soil_profiles.label: Indoor pots
soil_profiles.description: Orchid bark mix (orchid), standard potting mix (Bird of Paradise)
